answer:
In *Traité de la propriété*, Volume I, Chapter 2, Charles Comte argues that liberty is a prerequisite for the exercise of rights (including property rights, which he treats as flowing from natural liberty) and the fulfillment of duties.[^1]

He opens the chapter by framing the theme directly:

> On liberty considered as a condition of the exercise of all rights, and of the accomplishment of all duties.[^2]

Comte elaborates that recognizing oneself as a slave contradicts one's natural duties and rights:

> To acknowledge oneself a slave is not only to abdicate one's rights, it is moreover to renounce the accomplishment of one's duties; it is to recognize that one is bound to nothing, neither toward oneself, nor toward others; it is to proclaim a contradiction: for if, by his nature, man is bound to nothing, neither toward himself, nor toward others, how could he be bound to anything toward a master?[^3]

He concludes the argument by tying civil and political liberty explicitly to rights:

> Civil liberty and political liberty are therefore essential conditions of the exercise of all duties, and consequently of all rights.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.2, [chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.2, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-8ec508).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.2, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-3114c0).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.2, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-2efb96).
